metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Will Socialism Benefit the English People?, a debate published in 1884 by Henry Hyndman and Charles Bradlaugh, a British politician.
main_question:
We have no idea of that sort at all, neither do we think we can raise up a little oasis of co-operation in the midst of a great wilderness of competition. We have no such idea whatever. Every attempt made in this direction has been a failure, and we do not intend to try it again. [masked sentence describing a cause or reason] We are in no sense Utopians.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing a cause or reason]:
source_title: Will Socialism Benefit the English People?
answer_strings: They were failures because they did not take account of what was going on around them, of having an association which should dominate over all in the interest of all.
They were successes because they took account of what was going on around them, of having an association which should dominate over all in the interest of all.
Because the upper and middle classes have laid their hands on the endowments intended for the benefit of the poor, and taken them to their own advantage.
The very nature of our society is antithetical to such ideals.
The entrenched power of the landed aristocracy and the industrialists has stifled any attempt at collective organization.
passage: We have no idea of that sort at all, neither do we think we can raise up a little oasis of co-operation in the midst of a great wilderness of competition. We have no such idea whatever. Every attempt made in this direction has been a failure, and we do not intend to try it again. They were failures because they did not take account of what was going on around them, of having an association which should dominate over all in the interest of all. We are in no sense Utopians.

main_question:
(Hear, hear, and Oh.) I have been in hundreds of them. My speech will go amongst Lancashire and Yorkshire men who work in those mills, and who will know whether what I am saying is true or not. (Cheers.) In every new mil! built in the last twenty years the best resources of science have been utilised, [masked clause describing a cause or reason]. (Hear, hear.)

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a cause or reason]:
source_title: Will Socialism Benefit the English People?
answer_strings: because owners of capital have found that under comfortable conditions more is got from the labor, and therefore they do it
because workers have found that under harsh conditions less is yielded by their labor, and therefore they resist
for painting, or for music, or for sculpture, and if not, how will excellence in these be won
because we say hungry men make revolutions and riots, but they never made and organised revolution yet
because the mill owners wanted to maximize efficiency and profits
to meet the growing demand for speed and quality in textile production, reduce waste, and improve worker safety
passage: (Hear, hear, and Oh.) I have been in hundreds of them. My speech will go amongst Lancashire and Yorkshire men who work in those mills, and who will know whether what I am saying is true or not. (Cheers.) In every new mil! built in the last twenty years the best resources of science have been utilised, because owners of capital have found that under comfortable conditions more is got from the labor, and therefore they do it. (Hear, hear.)

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Will Socialism Benefit the English People?, a debate published in 1884 by Henry Hyndman and Charles Bradlaugh, a British politician.
main_question:
And with regard to this building society business which is brought forward, and the amount of land held by building societies, a great many of those lands are mortgaged heavily to the capitalist class; and in addition to that with regard to the large number of owners whom my opponent quotes from the Blue-books, I would ask him to look at the "Financial Reform Almanack," and see how those Blue-books have been fudged up, and how a single owner sometimes figures as eight or ten. [masked sentence revising an implied expectation] Suppose they get a full return for all the labor which they do, they would get in each year three or four times the amount of their building allotment.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]:
source_title: Will Socialism Benefit the English People?
answer_strings: But do you suppose that even those who hold building allotments are going to be dispossessed or injured?
But do you suppose that even those who hold building allotments are going to be enriched or benefited?
But who are the shareholders in the railroads : do they ever do any good in the world?
But even if we were to grant that they actually earned it.
Yet, the reality is that the returns are far lower than the inflated figures in the Blue‑books imply.
passage: And with regard to this building society business which is brought forward, and the amount of land held by building societies, a great many of those lands are mortgaged heavily to the capitalist class; and in addition to that with regard to the large number of owners whom my opponent quotes from the Blue-books, I would ask him to look at the "Financial Reform Almanack," and see how those Blue-books have been fudged up, and how a single owner sometimes figures as eight or ten. But do you suppose that even those who hold building allotments are going to be dispossessed or injured? Suppose they get a full return for all the labor which they do, they would get in each year three or four times the amount of their building allotment.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Will Socialism Benefit the English People?, a debate published in 1884 by Henry Hyndman and Charles Bradlaugh, a British politician.
main_question:
So infamous was the slavery under unrestricted competition that it was necessary to stop the degradation which was going on. Again, people were growing up in ignorance. Parents did not see that their children were educated in any way whatever. It became so serious to the community -so manifest was it that it was necessary to intervene, that the School Board was introduced, and it has not gone half far enough in my humble judgment, [masked clause revising an implied expectation]. There is an interference on the part of the State.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause revising an implied expectation]:
source_title: Will Socialism Benefit the English People?
answer_strings: but nevertheless the rights of parents to bring up their children in ignorance were interfered with by Parliament, and were put a stop to as far as they then could go
but nonetheless Parliament respected the rights of parents to not educate their children, and allowed them free rein
but that is not Socialism
but it is not common sense
to ensure that every child receives at least some basic instruction
that the State ought to have taken a more comprehensive approach to ensure equitable education for all children
passage: So infamous was the slavery under unrestricted competition that it was necessary to stop the degradation which was going on. Again, people were growing up in ignorance. Parents did not see that their children were educated in any way whatever. It became so serious to the community -so manifest was it that it was necessary to intervene, that the School Board was introduced, and it has not gone half far enough in my humble judgment, but nevertheless the rights of parents to bring up their children in ignorance were interfered with by Parliament, and were put a stop to as far as they then could go. There is an interference on the part of the State.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Will Socialism Benefit the English People?, a debate published in 1884 by Henry Hyndman and Charles Bradlaugh, a British politician.
main_question:
But I say that such a revolution will enhance individuality, it will relieve people from this crushing domination of a class, and will enable each man to exchange through Government banks and Government distributive centres. Then men may have and own the fruits of their labor whereby they may all benefit. [masked sentence describing an inference or effect] England wherein each man will work for all, where there will be free exchange of the fruits of labor without any profit, and where we shall hold up a really organised centre for mankind.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing an inference or effect]:
source_title: Will Socialism Benefit the English People?
answer_strings: Therefore I say what we look to is a thoroughly organised SOCIALISM.
Therefore I say what we look to is a thoroughly organized CAPITALISM.
This will lead to a society free from exploitation.
Thus, the people's prosperity will rise, and equality shall flourish for all.
passage: But I say that such a revolution will enhance individuality, it will relieve people from this crushing domination of a class, and will enable each man to exchange through Government banks and Government distributive centres. Then men may have and own the fruits of their labor whereby they may all benefit. Therefore I say what we look to is a thoroughly organised SOCIALISM. England wherein each man will work for all, where there will be free exchange of the fruits of labor without any profit, and where we shall hold up a really organised centre for mankind.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Will Socialism Benefit the English People?, a debate published in 1884 by Henry Hyndman and Charles Bradlaugh, a British politician.
main_question:
How is the distinction to be made between employment on skilled and unskilled labor? Individual effort regulates all this; State effort would crush it all. You talk about foreign produce. How are you going to get it? You will have no markets here. ("Why not?") Why, [masked clause describing a condition or proviso], nobody can sell to anybody. (Laughter.)

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]:
source_title: Will Socialism Benefit the English People?
answer_strings: if all things are owned by everybody
if everything is privately held
unless you succeed by argument
unless you have something to offer
if the state forbids private exchange,
passage: How is the distinction to be made between employment on skilled and unskilled labor? Individual effort regulates all this; State effort would crush it all. You talk about foreign produce. How are you going to get it? You will have no markets here. ("Why not?") Why, if all things are owned by everybody, nobody can sell to anybody. (Laughter.)

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Will Socialism Benefit the English People?, a debate published in 1884 by Henry Hyndman and Charles Bradlaugh, a British politician.
main_question:
It is the best educated and organised and capable men who have always made revolutions in our country. The revolu tion of 1641 to 1649, one of the greatest revolutions in history, was made in the interest of the middle classes, but how was it made? It was made by strong, stalwart, well-conducted, well-fed men. I say those revolutions were beneficial, and I say that such a revolution to-day, [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]. (Cheers.)

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]:
source_title: Will Socialism Benefit the English People?
answer_strings: although God forbid it should come by force, I trust will come with the organised education of all
although God forbid it should come by force, I trust will come without the organised education of all
even if wrongfully, we have tried for thirty years to awaken men to the knowledge of it
if it is not accompanied by the same level of education and organization as before
though it may also sow discord and destabilise the social fabric
passage: It is the best educated and organised and capable men who have always made revolutions in our country. The revolu tion of 1641 to 1649, one of the greatest revolutions in history, was made in the interest of the middle classes, but how was it made? It was made by strong, stalwart, well-conducted, well-fed men. I say those revolutions were beneficial, and I say that such a revolution to-day, although God forbid it should come by force, I trust will come with the organised education of all. (Cheers.)
